1	{"body":"### Vendor

claude

### Kind of change

kind:urgent-fix — the vendor unilaterally broke compat; the connector is currently broken in the field

### What changed (or appears to have changed) on the vendor's side

- The Claude Code connector did not parse the `expiresAt` field (Unix
  millisecond timestamp) present in the macOS Keychain JSON for
  `Claude Code-credentials`. Expired access tokens were forwarded to
  the API unchanged, triggering HTTP 401 responses.
- With 15-second polling, Anthropic's rate limiter eventually switches
  from 401 to HTTP 429, which persists indefinitely. Without a sticky
  auth-failure flag, each polling cycle overwrote `lastError` with
  `http_429`, hiding the actionable "token expired" diagnostic behind
  a misleading "rate limited" surface.
- `lastKnownMetrics` was being cleared on auth errors, destroying
  still-valid window data (token expiry invalidates the ability to
  refresh the numbers, not the numbers themselves).
- First observed: 2026-05-19 by @fcamblor via a user report showing
  `lastError.type: http_429` and a `resetAt` 22 days in the past.

### Evidence

- Affected user's `usages.json` at the time of report:
  `lastError.type: http_429`, `resetAt: 2026-04-27` (user last ran
  the `claude` CLI on 2026-04-27; token expired within hours; polling
  never stopped).
- UI screenshot: all metrics showing `???` (stale `resetAt` combined
  with wiped `lastKnownMetrics`).
- The `expiresAt` field exists in the Keychain JSON; the locator was
  not reading it.

### App version impact

Not applicable (kind:urgent-fix).

### Affected since (kind:urgent-fix only)

2026-04-27 ~00:00 UTC

### Contract acknowledgement

- [x] I understand the existing `docs/vendors/claude.md` will be
  re-dated and superseded samples kept, with a Change log entry.
- [x] I understand merge requires ≥ 1 tester for kind:urgent-fix,
  with follow-up confirmations expected post-release.","comments":[{"id":"IC_kwDOSFLrJs8AAAABC3bpXg","author":{"login":"fcamblor"},"authorAssociation":"OWNER","body":"## Triage decision

**Scope**: In scope — `type:vendor-evolution` / `kind:urgent-fix` on vendor `claude`.

**Existence check**: `docs/vendors/claude.md` exists and `ClaudeCodePlugin` is registered in `VendorRegistry`. No doc or registration gap.

**Urgency confirmation**: The connector is confirmed broken in production as of 2026-04-27. The `expiresAt` field in the macOS Keychain JSON was not being read, causing expired tokens to be forwarded to the API (HTTP 401 → HTTP 429 cascade). Metrics were also being wiped on auth errors, hiding still-valid window data. This is an active field breakage, not an announced future deprecation.

**Constraints**:

- The fix must read `expiresAt` from the Keychain JSON and surface a sticky `token_expired` error (or equivalent) before the 401 path is exercised.
- `lastKnownMetrics` must be preserved on auth errors — only the refresh attempt should be blocked, not the cached data.
- `docs/vendors/claude.md` must be re-dated with a Change log entry; any superseded samples must be annotated `superseded by <date>` and kept.
- Tester plans to cover: at minimum one account with an expired token (reproduce locally or await natural expiry on a free-plan account).

**Merge threshold**: `kind:urgent-fix` — ≥ 1 tester with a clean attached connector log. Issue stays open post-release for follow-up confirmations.

**Decision**: approved → `phase:approved`. The form pre-fills the build SHA, vendor, and macOS version, and on submit it
opens this issue in your browser with the comment pre-written + reveals the
connector log in Finder so you can drag-attach it.

**Manual fallback:** copy the sign-off template from `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`,
fill it in, attach the log file from `~/.cache/ai-usages-tracker/claude-usages-connector.log`.

The build sanitizes secrets (tokens, API keys, emails) before writing to the log;
if you spot anything that looks confidential, flag it in your comment without

file.content:
{"body":"### Vendor

claude

### Kind of change

kind:urgent-fix — the vendor unilaterally broke compat; the connector is currently broken in the field

### What changed (or appears to have changed) on the vendor's side

- The Claude Code connector did not parse the `expiresAt` field (Unix
  millisecond timestamp) present in the macOS Keychain JSON for
  `Claude Code-credentials`. Expired access tokens were forwarded to
  the API unchanged, triggering HTTP 401 responses.
- With 15-second polling, Anthropic's rate limiter eventually switches
  from 401 to HTTP 429, which persists indefinitely. Without a sticky
  auth-failure flag, each polling cycle overwrote `lastError` with
  `http_429`, hiding the actionable "token expired" diagnostic behind
  a misleading "rate limited" surface.
- `lastKnownMetrics` was being cleared on auth errors, destroying
  still-valid window data (token expiry invalidates the ability to
  refresh the numbers, not the numbers themselves).
- First observed: 2026-05-19 by @fcamblor via a user report showing
  `lastError.type: http_429` and a `resetAt` 22 days in the past.

### Evidence

- Affected user's `usages.json` at the time of report:
  `lastError.type: http_429`, `resetAt: 2026-04-27` (user last ran
  the `claude` CLI on 2026-04-27; token expired within hours; polling
  never stopped).
- UI screenshot: all metrics showing `???` (stale `resetAt` combined
  with wiped `lastKnownMetrics`).
- The `expiresAt` field exists in the Keychain JSON; the locator was
  not reading it.

### App version impact

Not applicable (kind:urgent-fix).

### Affected since (kind:urgent-fix only)

2026-04-27 ~00:00 UTC

### Contract acknowledgement

- [x] I understand the existing `docs/vendors/claude.md` will be
  re-dated and superseded samples kept, with a Change log entry.
- [x] I understand merge requires ≥ 1 tester for kind:urgent-fix,
  with follow-up confirmations expected post-release.","comments":[{"id":"IC_kwDOSFLrJs8AAAABC3bpXg","author":{"login":"fcamblor"},"authorAssociation":"OWNER","body":"## Triage decision

**Scope**: In scope — `type:vendor-evolution` / `kind:urgent-fix` on vendor `claude`.

**Existence check**: `docs/vendors/claude.md` exists and `ClaudeCodePlugin` is registered in `VendorRegistry`. No doc or registration gap.

**Urgency confirmation**: The connector is confirmed broken in production as of 2026-04-27. The `expiresAt` field in the macOS Keychain JSON was not being read, causing expired tokens to be forwarded to the API (HTTP 401 → HTTP 429 cascade). Metrics were also being wiped on auth errors, hiding still-valid window data. This is an active field breakage, not an announced future deprecation.

**Constraints**:

- The fix must read `expiresAt` from the Keychain JSON and surface a sticky `token_expired` error (or equivalent) before the 401 path is exercised.
- `lastKnownMetrics` must be preserved on auth errors — only the refresh attempt should be blocked, not the cached data.
- `docs/vendors/claude.md` must be re-dated with a Change log entry; any superseded samples must be annotated `superseded by <date>` and kept.
- Tester plans to cover: at minimum one account with an expired token (reproduce locally or await natural expiry on a free-plan account).

**Merge threshold**: `kind:urgent-fix` — ≥ 1 tester with a clean attached connector log. Issue stays open post-release for follow-up confirmations.

**Decision**: approved → `phase:approved`. Built at: 2026-05-19 23:29 UTC

- [Download AI-Usages-Tracker-claude-bc45e460.dmg](https://github.com/fcamblor/mac-ai-trackers/releases/download/assistant-build-pr45-claude-bc45e460/AI-Usages-Tracker-claude-bc45e460.dmg) — SHA-256 `ecaf2de5de978d00645c0a50faff37b66c3db7ccf843808ddd5220c107031a9d`

Builds are ad-hoc signed only. First launch: right-click → Open to bypass Gatekeeper.

### Submitting feedback

**Recommended:** open the app, click the "🧪 Tester feedback" banner in the popover.
The form pre-fills the build SHA, vendor, and macOS version, and on submit it
opens this issue in your browser with the comment pre-written + reveals the
connector log in Finder so you can drag-attach it.

**Manual fallback:** copy the sign-off template from `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`,
fill it in, attach the log file from `~/.cache/ai-usages-tracker/claude-usages-connector.log`.

content.thinking:
Now I have all the information I need. Let me analyze what I found:

**Phase A — Vérification de la portée et de la phase**
- Labels: `type:vendor-evolution`, `kind:urgent-fix`, `phase:testing`
- Un seul label `phase:*` → pas de violation de mutex
- L'issue est bien en `phase:testing` → le skill est approprié ✅

**Phase B — Localiser le dernier build**
- Le commentaire sticky `<!-- assistant-build:sticky -->` indique:
  - Short SHA: `bc45e460`
  - Full SHA: `bc45e460fc081b35a3bd02901a82a9951b96ff41`

**Phase C — Scanner les sign-offs de testeurs**
@robinlopez a confirmé les tests avec un sign-off complet, incluant les détails du plan Pro, la version macOS 26.2, et la vérification des métriques d'utilisation et de détection de compte actif.

En analysant ce sign-off, je remarque que le SHA du build (`1f26daba`) diffère du dernier SHA sticky (`bc45e460`), mais selon les règles de validation, les confirmations restent valides à travers les rebuilds tant que le log attaché s'audite correctement contre la documentation du vendor. Le log étant marqué comme présent, je peux compter cette confirmation sans bloquer sur la différence de SHA.

Toutes les cases requises sont cochées, y compris l'option optionnelle pour la bannière d'incident. Maintenant je dois auditer le log attaché en le comparant contre la section `Sanitized fields` de la documentation Claude pour vérifier qu'aucune donnée sensible n'a été exposée — je remarque déjà une ligne potentiellement problématique avec l'email `robin.lopez@4sh.fr` qui pourrait violer les règles de sanitization.

content.text: Je dois maintenant lire le doc vendor Claude pour vérifier les champs sanitisés.

content.content:
1	# Claude Code
2	
3	> **Last verified:** 2026-05-19 by @fcamblor on Claude Code Pro plan
4	
5	<!--
6	  Read `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md` first; it defines the shape of
7	  this document. If this file disagrees with the contract, the contract
8	  wins.
9	-->
10	
11	## Endpoints
12	
13	_verified: 2026-05-07_
14	
15	| Method | URL | Headers | Timeout | Response Content-Type |
16	|--------|-----|---------|---------|-----------------------|
17	| GET    | `https://api.anthropic.com/api/oauth/usage` | `Authorization: Bearer <oauth>`, `anthropic-beta: oauth-2025-04-20` | 5s | `application/json` |
18	| GET    | `https://api.anthropic.com/v1/organizations/cost_report` | `x-api-key: <admin_key>`, `anthropic-version: 2023-06-01` | unimplemented | `application/json` |
19	
20	The `anthropic-beta` value matches the rollout date documented when the
21	OAuth-app usage endpoint was made available.
22	
23	The organization cost endpoint is documented by Anthropic's Admin API but
24	is not implemented by this tracker. It requires an Admin API key
25	(`sk-ant-admin...`), not a standard API key (`sk-ant-api...`), and Anthropic
26	documents the Admin API as unavailable for individual accounts.
27	
28	## Credential sources
29	
30	_verified: 2026-05-19_
31	
32	Cascade (read-only — the app never writes any of these):
33	
34	1. **macOS Keychain entry** `Claude Code-credentials` written by the
35	   `claude` CLI. Value is JSON of the form
36	   `{"claudeAiOauth":{"accessToken":"sk-ant-oat01-...","refreshToken":"...","expiresAt":1715000000000}}`.
37	   `expiresAt` is a Unix millisecond timestamp (Node.js `Date.now()`
38	   convention — Claude Code CLI is a Node/Bun program).
39	2. **Unsupported diagnostic only:** macOS Keychain entry `Claude Code`
40	   may contain a standard Claude API key (`sk-ant-api...`) when Claude Code
41	   is configured for API-key auth. The tracker detects this state so it can
42	   surface `api_key_usage_unsupported` instead of a generic token error, but
43	   it does not use that key for usage fetching because Anthropic's usage/cost
44	   reporting requires an Admin API key.
45	
46	The `claude` CLI owns the lifecycle. The locator never calls
47	`SecItemAdd`, never invokes `claude auth`, never writes the file at
48	`~/.claude.json`. The locator also never performs the OAuth refresh
49	flow — if the access token has expired, the only way to get a new one
50	is for the user to invoke the `claude` CLI, which uses the stored
51	refresh token. **An access token whose owner stops using the CLI dies
52	within hours and stays dead until the CLI is invoked again** — see the
53	"Stale-token failure mode" section below.
54	
55	### Local expiry pre-check
56	
57	Before sending each request, the locator parses `expiresAt` from the
58	keychain JSON and short-circuits with `ClaudeAuthError.tokenExpired` if
59	the token is past its expiry (with a 60-second skew margin to avoid
60	racing the boundary). A missing or unparseable `expiresAt` skips the
61	local check and falls through to the HTTP layer — a real 401 then
62	surfaces as `token_expired` (see Error catalog).
63	
64	This pre-check exists specifically to break the chronic 401→429 loop
65	described in the Stale-token failure mode below.
66	
67	The active account email is read from `~/.claude.json` (`oauthAccount.emailAddress`).
68	
69	## Stale-token failure mode
70	
71	_verified: 2026-05-19_
72	
73	A user who logs into the `claude` CLI once (typically to enable this
74	app) and then stops using the CLI exhibits a distinctive failure mode:
75	
76	1. The keychain `accessToken` expires within hours (no refresh because
77	   the CLI is never invoked).
78	2. Subsequent calls to `/api/oauth/usage` return HTTP 401.
79	3. If the app keeps polling on the expired token (every 15s), Anthropic
80	   eventually switches from 401 to **HTTP 429** (rate_limit_error). The
81	   429 persists indefinitely as long as the polling continues — it is
82	   not a transient throttle, it is a downstream effect of the 401.
83	4. Without a sticky auth-failure flag, each 429 overwrites the
84	   connector's `lastError` to `http_429` and the actionable diagnostic
85	   ("token expired, re-run the CLI") is lost behind a misleading
86	   "rate limited" surface.
87	
88	The fix has three pieces:
89	
90	- **Locator-side pre-check.** Parsing `expiresAt` and short-circuiting
91	  with `tokenExpired` skips the HTTP call entirely when we already know
92	  the bearer is dead, which prevents the request that would have
93	  produced the 429 cascade in the first place.
94	- **Preserve `lastKnownMetrics` on `token_expired`.** Token expiry
95	  invalidates our ability to *refresh* the numbers, not the numbers
96	  themselves. A 5h session at 42% is still 42% — the user actually
97	  consumed that. Values whose window has not yet rolled stay accurate
98	  until their `resetAt`; once `resetAt` is in the past, the UI's
99	  `isUnknown` logic naturally degrades the row to `???` without any
100	  data wipe on our side.
101	- **Sticky auth-failure flag.** Once a 401 (or local `tokenExpired`)
102	  has fired, subsequent 429s are re-attributed to `token_expired` so
103	  the auth signal is not overwritten by the cascade. The flag clears
104	  on any HTTP 200.
105	
106	The fix only addresses the diagnostic, not the root cause: the user
107	must still re-invoke the `claude` CLI for the OAuth refresh to happen.
108	
109	## Sanitized fields
110	
111	_verified: 2026-05-07_
112	
113	Drives the leakage test in `Tests/Fixtures/claude-full-payload.json`.
114	
115	| Location | Field path | Redaction style |
116	|----------|-----------|-----------------|
117	| Header   | `Authorization` value | full removal (`<redacted>`) |
118	| Header   | `anthropic-beta` value | preserved (no secret) |
119	| Body     | any `*token*` / `*key*` / `*secret*` / `*password*` / `*credential*` key | full removal |
120	| Body     | any `*email*` key | full removal |
121	| Message  | `[A-Za-z0-9._+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+` matches | replace with `<email>` |
122	| Body     | `request-id` (response header) | preserved (diagnostic, no secret) |
123	
124	The Claude usage endpoint response itself does not include tokens or
125	emails — the field-name based default-deny is defensive against future
126	schema changes.
127	
128	## Plan variants observed
129	
130	_verified: 2026-05-07_
131	
132	### Pro plan (verified by tester)
133	
134	```jsonc
135	<!-- captured: 2026-05-07, plan: Pro, login: redacted -->
136	{
137	  "five_hour": {
138	    "utilization": 50.0,
139	    "resets_at": "2026-05-07T00:29:59.571486+00:00"
140	  },
141	  "seven_day": {
142	    "utilization": 13.0,
143	    "resets_at": "2026-05-12T22:00:00.571503+00:00"
144	  },
145	  "seven_day_oauth_apps": null,
146	  "seven_day_opus": null,
147	  "seven_day_sonnet": {
148	    "utilization": 2.0,
149	    "resets_at": "2026-05-12T22:00:00.571510+00:00"
150	  },
151	  "seven_day_cowork": null,
152	  "seven_day_omelette": {
153	    "utilization": 0.0,
154	    "resets_at": null
155	  },
156	  "tangelo": null,
157	  "iguana_necktie": null,
158	  "omelette_promotional": null,
159	  "extra_usage": {
160	    "is_enabled": false,
161	    "monthly_limit": null,
162	    "used_credits": null,
163	    "utilization": null,
164	    "currency": null
165	  }
166	}
167	```
168	
169	Unknown top-level keys such as `seven_day_omelette`, `tangelo`,
170	`iguana_necktie`, and `omelette_promotional` are ignored by design.
171	`extra_usage` is consumed when enabled and emitted as
172	`.payAsYouGo("Extra usage spent", used_credits / 100, currency)`.
173	
174	### Max plan (assumed, not yet verified by tester)
175	
176	Same shape as Pro with additional `seven_day_opus` block populated
177	instead of `null`. To be confirmed by a tester running on Max.
178	
179	### Free plan (assumed, not yet verified by tester)
180	
181	Reportedly returns the same shape with all `seven_day_*` blocks `null`
182	and `five_hour.utilization` capped on a tighter quota.
183	
184	## Metric semantics
185	
186	_verified: 2026-05-20_
187	
188	| Swift metric | Source field | Reset cadence | Unit | Edge cases |
189	|--------------|--------------|---------------|------|------------|
190	| `.timeWindow("5h sessions (all models)")` | `five_hour.utilization` / `five_hour.resets_at` | rolling 5-hour window (300 minutes) | percent | `resets_at: null` → emit with `resetAt: nil`; missing block → metric omitted |
191	| `.timeWindow("Weekly (all models)")` | `seven_day.utilization` / `seven_day.resets_at` | weekly (10080 minutes) | percent | same |
192	| `.timeWindow("Weekly Sonnet")` | `seven_day_sonnet.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |
193	| `.timeWindow("Weekly Opus")` | `seven_day_opus.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |
194	| `.payAsYouGo("Extra usage spent")` | `extra_usage.used_credits` / `extra_usage.currency` | n/a | currency amount | emitted only when `extra_usage.is_enabled == true` and `used_credits` is numeric; API reports cents, tracker stores dollars |
195	
196	Window durations are not returned by the API — they are fixed by
197	Anthropic's rate-limit policy and hard-coded in the connector
198	(`sessionWindowMinutes = 300`, `weeklyWindowMinutes = 10080`).
199	
200	## Time semantics
201	
202	_verified: 2026-05-07_
203	
204	- Reset values arrive as ISO 8601 datetimes with sub-second precision:
205	  `2026-05-07T00:29:59.571486+00:00`.
206	- Connector strips sub-second precision via `normalizeISO8601(_:)` so
207	  downstream consumers can rely on the standard formatter without
208	  enabling fractional-seconds parsing.
209	
210	## Error catalog
211	
212	_verified: 2026-05-19_
213	
214	| Source | Outcome | Connector response |
215	|--------|---------|--------------------|
216	| Locator | `ClaudeAuthError.tokenExpired` (pre-check) | surface `token_expired`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true`; arm sticky auth-failure flag; skip HTTP entirely |
217	| Locator | `ClaudeAuthError.apiKeyOnlyUnsupported` | surface `api_key_usage_unsupported`; no metric refresh |
218	| Locator | other `ClaudeAuthError` (keychain missing / denied / parse) | surface `token_error` |
219	| HTTP   | 200 | parse, emit metrics, refresh `lastKnownMetrics`, clear sticky auth-failure flag |
220	| HTTP   | 401 | surface `token_expired`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true`; arm sticky auth-failure flag |
221	| HTTP   | 429 (auth-failure flag armed) | re-attribute to `token_expired`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true` |
222	| HTTP   | 429 (no recent auth failure) | surface `http_429`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true` |
223	| HTTP   | other | surface `http_<code>`; do not refresh metrics |
224	
225	A `parse_error` is surfaced if a 200 response cannot be decoded as JSON
226	or contains no known window block.
227	
228	`lastKnownMetrics` is intentionally **preserved** across auth failures:
229	an expired bearer means we can no longer refresh the data, but the
230	values we previously read are still semantically valid until their
231	window's `resetAt` naturally lapses. Wiping them would destroy
232	information that is still correct; instead the UI's `isUnknown` logic
233	collapses each metric to `???` on its own schedule (per-row), while
234	the `token_expired` error type drives any global "re-auth required"
235	banner.
236	
237	## Status page
238	
239	_verified: 2026-05-12_
240	
241	| URL | Format | Component filter |
242	|-----|--------|-----------------|
243	| `https://status.claude.com/api/v2/incidents/unresolved.json` | statuspage.io v2 | none — all incidents on this page are Anthropic-specific |
244	
245	`status.anthropic.com` 302-redirects to `status.claude.com`; the
246	connector points at the canonical destination so it does not depend on
247	`URLSession` following redirects.
248	
249	`ClaudeStatusConnector` fetches unresolved incidents and surfaces any
250	incident whose `impact` is not `"none"`. Impact maps directly to
251	`OutageSeverity` (`critical`, `major`, `minor`, `maintenance`); the
252	incident `shortlink` becomes the clickable href in the UI.
253	
254	## Known unknowns
255	
256	- Max plan shape with active `seven_day_opus` quota — assumed identical
257	  to Pro plus a populated Opus block. Pending tester verification.
258	- Free plan shape — assumed Pro-shaped with reduced quotas. Pending
259	  tester verification.
260	- Enterprise / Team plans — completely unverified. Behavior unknown.
261	- The `extra_usage` block's `is_enabled: true` payload — never observed
262	  by the maintainer. Pending tester verification.
263	
264	## Source references
265	
266	- Anthropic API documentation, OAuth usage endpoint
267	  (https://docs.anthropic.com/) (retrieved 2026-05-07).
268	- Anthropic Usage and Cost Admin API documentation
269	  (https://docs.anthropic.com/en/api/data-usage-cost-api) (retrieved
270	  2026-05-20).
271	- Maintainer's own captures during development of `ClaudeCodeConnector`.
272	
273	## Change log
274	
275	- 2026-05-07 — initial capture: Pro plan response shape, fixed window
276	  durations, ISO 8601 with sub-second precision, beta header
277	  `oauth-2025-04-20`.
278	- 2026-05-19 — documented `expiresAt` keychain field (Unix ms epoch),
279	  added local expiry pre-check in the locator and explicit
280	  `token_expired` mapping for both the pre-check and HTTP 401.
281	  `lastKnownMetrics` is preserved across auth failures (data validity
282	  is independent of token validity — the UI's `isUnknown` logic
283	  handles per-row staleness on `resetAt` lapse). A sticky
284	  auth-failure flag re-attributes subsequent 429s to `token_expired`
285	  until a successful 200 disarms it, preventing the chronic 401→429
286	  cascade from masking the auth diagnostic. Documented the
287	  Stale-token failure mode that motivated the change.
288	- 2026-05-20 — document standard API-key auth limitation and parse
289	  `extra_usage` as a pay-as-you-go metric.

# Claude Code

> **Last verified:** 2026-05-19 by @fcamblor on Claude Code Pro plan

<!--
  Read `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md` first; it defines the shape of
  this document. If this file disagrees with the contract, the contract
  wins.
-->

## Endpoints

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Method | URL | Headers | Timeout | Response Content-Type |
|--------|-----|---------|---------|-----------------------|
| GET    | `https://api.anthropic.com/api/oauth/usage` | `Authorization: Bearer <oauth>`, `anthropic-beta: oauth-2025-04-20` | 5s | `application/json` |
| GET    | `https://api.anthropic.com/v1/organizations/cost_report` | `x-api-key: <admin_key>`, `anthropic-version: 2023-06-01` | unimplemented | `application/json` |

The `anthropic-beta` value matches the rollout date documented when the
OAuth-app usage endpoint was made available.

The organization cost endpoint is documented by Anthropic's Admin API but
is not implemented by this tracker. It requires an Admin API key
(`sk-ant-admin...`), not a standard API key (`sk-ant-api...`), and Anthropic
documents the Admin API as unavailable for individual accounts.

## Credential sources

_verified: 2026-05-19_

Cascade (read-only — the app never writes any of these):

1. **macOS Keychain entry** `Claude Code-credentials` written by the
   `claude` CLI. Value is JSON of the form
   `{"claudeAiOauth":{"accessToken":"sk-ant-oat01-...","refreshToken":"...","expiresAt":1715000000000}}`.
   `expiresAt` is a Unix millisecond timestamp (Node.js `Date.now()`
   convention — Claude Code CLI is a Node/Bun program).
2. **Unsupported diagnostic only:** macOS Keychain entry `Claude Code`
   may contain a standard Claude API key (`sk-ant-api...`) when Claude Code
   is configured for API-key auth. The tracker detects this state so it can
   surface `api_key_usage_unsupported` instead of a generic token error, but
   it does not use that key for usage fetching because Anthropic's usage/cost
   reporting requires an Admin API key.

The `claude` CLI owns the lifecycle. The locator never calls
`SecItemAdd`, never invokes `claude auth`, never writes the file at
`~/.claude.json`. The locator also never performs the OAuth refresh
flow — if the access token has expired, the only way to get a new one
is for the user to invoke the `claude` CLI, which uses the stored
refresh token. **An access token whose owner stops using the CLI dies
within hours and stays dead until the CLI is invoked again** — see the
"Stale-token failure mode" section below.

### Local expiry pre-check

Before sending each request, the locator parses `expiresAt` from the
keychain JSON and short-circuits with `ClaudeAuthError.tokenExpired` if
the token is past its expiry (with a 60-second skew margin to avoid
racing the boundary). A missing or unparseable `expiresAt` skips the
local check and falls through to the HTTP layer — a real 401 then
surfaces as `token_expired` (see Error catalog).

This pre-check exists specifically to break the chronic 401→429 loop
described in the Stale-token failure mode below.

The active account email is read from `~/.claude.json` (`oauthAccount.emailAddress`).

## Stale-token failure mode

_verified: 2026-05-19_

A user who logs into the `claude` CLI once (typically to enable this
app) and then stops using the CLI exhibits a distinctive failure mode:

1. The keychain `accessToken` expires within hours (no refresh because
   the CLI is never invoked).
2. Subsequent calls to `/api/oauth/usage` return HTTP 401.
3. If the app keeps polling on the expired token (every 15s), Anthropic
   eventually switches from 401 to **HTTP 429** (rate_limit_error). The
   429 persists indefinitely as long as the polling continues — it is
   not a transient throttle, it is a downstream effect of the 401.
4. Without a sticky auth-failure flag, each 429 overwrites the
   connector's `lastError` to `http_429` and the actionable diagnostic
   ("token expired, re-run the CLI") is lost behind a misleading
   "rate limited" surface.

The fix has three pieces:

- **Locator-side pre-check.** Parsing `expiresAt` and short-circuiting
  with `tokenExpired` skips the HTTP call entirely when we already know
  the bearer is dead, which prevents the request that would have
  produced the 429 cascade in the first place.
- **Preserve `lastKnownMetrics` on `token_expired`.** Token expiry
  invalidates our ability to *refresh* the numbers, not the numbers
  themselves. A 5h session at 42% is still 42% — the user actually
  consumed that. Values whose window has not yet rolled stay accurate
  until their `resetAt`; once `resetAt` is in the past, the UI's
  `isUnknown` logic naturally degrades the row to `???` without any
  data wipe on our side.
- **Sticky auth-failure flag.** Once a 401 (or local `tokenExpired`)
  has fired, subsequent 429s are re-attributed to `token_expired` so
  the auth signal is not overwritten by the cascade. The flag clears
  on any HTTP 200.

The fix only addresses the diagnostic, not the root cause: the user
must still re-invoke the `claude` CLI for the OAuth refresh to happen.

## Sanitized fields

_verified: 2026-05-07_

Drives the leakage test in `Tests/Fixtures/claude-full-payload.json`.

| Location | Field path | Redaction style |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| Header   | `Authorization` value | full removal (`<redacted>`) |
| Header   | `anthropic-beta` value | preserved (no secret) |
| Body     | any `*token*` / `*key*` / `*secret*` / `*password*` / `*credential*` key | full removal |
| Body     | any `*email*` key | full removal |
| Message  | `[A-Za-z0-9._+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+` matches | replace with `<email>` |
| Body     | `request-id` (response header) | preserved (diagnostic, no secret) |

The Claude usage endpoint response itself does not include tokens or
emails — the field-name based default-deny is defensive against future
schema changes.

## Plan variants observed

_verified: 2026-05-07_

### Pro plan (verified by tester)

```jsonc
<!-- captured: 2026-05-07, plan: Pro, login: redacted -->
{
  "five_hour": {
    "utilization": 50.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-07T00:29:59.571486+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day": {
    "utilization": 13.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-12T22:00:00.571503+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day_oauth_apps": null,
  "seven_day_opus": null,
  "seven_day_sonnet": {
    "utilization": 2.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-12T22:00:00.571510+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day_cowork": null,
  "seven_day_omelette": {
    "utilization": 0.0,
    "resets_at": null
  },
  "tangelo": null,
  "iguana_necktie": null,
  "omelette_promotional": null,
  "extra_usage": {
    "is_enabled": false,
    "monthly_limit": null,
    "used_credits": null,
    "utilization": null,
    "currency": null
  }
}
```

Unknown top-level keys such as `seven_day_omelette`, `tangelo`,
`iguana_necktie`, and `omelette_promotional` are ignored by design.
`extra_usage` is consumed when enabled and emitted as
`.payAsYouGo("Extra usage spent", used_credits / 100, currency)`.

### Max plan (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

Same shape as Pro with additional `seven_day_opus` block populated
instead of `null`. To be confirmed by a tester running on Max.

### Free plan (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

Reportedly returns the same shape with all `seven_day_*` blocks `null`
and `five_hour.utilization` capped on a tighter quota.

## Metric semantics

_verified: 2026-05-20_

| Swift metric | Source field | Reset cadence | Unit | Edge cases |
|--------------|--------------|---------------|------|------------|
| `.timeWindow("5h sessions (all models)")` | `five_hour.utilization` / `five_hour.resets_at` | rolling 5-hour window (300 minutes) | percent | `resets_at: null` → emit with `resetAt: nil`; missing block → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly (all models)")` | `seven_day.utilization` / `seven_day.resets_at` | weekly (10080 minutes) | percent | same |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly Sonnet")` | `seven_day_sonnet.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly Opus")` | `seven_day_opus.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |
| `.payAsYouGo("Extra usage spent")` | `extra_usage.used_credits` / `extra_usage.currency` | n/a | currency amount | emitted only when `extra_usage.is_enabled == true` and `used_credits` is numeric; API reports cents, tracker stores dollars |

Window durations are not returned by the API — they are fixed by
Anthropic's rate-limit policy and hard-coded in the connector
(`sessionWindowMinutes = 300`, `weeklyWindowMinutes = 10080`).

## Time semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

- Reset values arrive as ISO 8601 datetimes with sub-second precision:
  `2026-05-07T00:29:59.571486+00:00`.
- Connector strips sub-second precision via `normalizeISO8601(_:)` so
  downstream consumers can rely on the standard formatter without
  enabling fractional-seconds parsing.

## Error catalog

_verified: 2026-05-19_

| Source | Outcome | Connector response |
|--------|---------|--------------------|
| Locator | `ClaudeAuthError.tokenExpired` (pre-check) | surface `token_expired`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true`; arm sticky auth-failure flag; skip HTTP entirely |
| Locator | `ClaudeAuthError.apiKeyOnlyUnsupported` | surface `api_key_usage_unsupported`; no metric refresh |
| Locator | other `ClaudeAuthError` (keychain missing / denied / parse) | surface `token_error` |
| HTTP   | 200 | parse, emit metrics, refresh `lastKnownMetrics`, clear sticky auth-failure flag |
| HTTP   | 401 | surface `token_expired`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true`; arm sticky auth-failure flag |
| HTTP   | 429 (auth-failure flag armed) | re-attribute to `token_expired`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true` |
| HTTP   | 429 (no recent auth failure) | surface `http_429`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true` |
| HTTP   | other | surface `http_<code>`; do not refresh metrics |

A `parse_error` is surfaced if a 200 response cannot be decoded as JSON
or contains no known window block.

`lastKnownMetrics` is intentionally **preserved** across auth failures:
an expired bearer means we can no longer refresh the data, but the
values we previously read are still semantically valid until their
window's `resetAt` naturally lapses. Wiping them would destroy
information that is still correct; instead the UI's `isUnknown` logic
collapses each metric to `???` on its own schedule (per-row), while
the `token_expired` error type drives any global "re-auth required"
banner.

## Status page

_verified: 2026-05-12_

| URL | Format | Component filter |
|-----|--------|-----------------|
| `https://status.claude.com/api/v2/incidents/unresolved.json` | statuspage.io v2 | none — all incidents on this page are Anthropic-specific |

`status.anthropic.com` 302-redirects to `status.claude.com`; the
connector points at the canonical destination so it does not depend on
`URLSession` following redirects.

`ClaudeStatusConnector` fetches unresolved incidents and surfaces any
incident whose `impact` is not `"none"`. Impact maps directly to
`OutageSeverity` (`critical`, `major`, `minor`, `maintenance`); the
incident `shortlink` becomes the clickable href in the UI.

## Known unknowns

- Max plan shape with active `seven_day_opus` quota — assumed identical
  to Pro plus a populated Opus block. Pending tester verification.
- Free plan shape — assumed Pro-shaped with reduced quotas. Pending
  tester verification.
- Enterprise / Team plans — completely unverified. Behavior unknown.
- The `extra_usage` block's `is_enabled: true` payload — never observed
  by the maintainer. Pending tester verification.

## Source references

- Anthropic API documentation, OAuth usage endpoint
  (https://docs.anthropic.com/) (retrieved 2026-05-07).
- Anthropic Usage and Cost Admin API documentation
  (https://docs.anthropic.com/en/api/data-usage-cost-api) (retrieved
  2026-05-20).
- Maintainer's own captures during development of `ClaudeCodeConnector`.

## Change log

- 2026-05-07 — initial capture: Pro plan response shape, fixed window
  durations, ISO 8601 with sub-second precision, beta header
  `oauth-2025-04-20`.
- 2026-05-19 — documented `expiresAt` keychain field (Unix ms epoch),
  added local expiry pre-check in the locator and explicit
  `token_expired` mapping for both the pre-check and HTTP 401.
  `lastKnownMetrics` is preserved across auth failures (data validity
  is independent of token validity — the UI's `isUnknown` logic
  handles per-row staleness on `resetAt` lapse). A sticky
  auth-failure flag re-attributes subsequent 429s to `token_expired`
  until a successful 200 disarms it, preventing the chronic 401→429
  cascade from masking the auth diagnostic. Documented the
  Stale-token failure mode that motivated the change.
- 2026-05-20 — document standard API-key auth limitation and parse
  `extra_usage` as a pay-as-you-go metric.

   it does not use that key for usage fetching because Anthropic's usage/cost
   reporting requires an Admin API key.

The `claude` CLI owns the lifecycle. The locator never calls
`SecItemAdd`, never invokes `claude auth`, never writes the file at
`~/.claude.json`. The locator also never performs the OAuth refresh
flow — if the access token has expired, the only way to get a new one
is for the user to invoke the `claude` CLI, which uses the stored
refresh token. **An access token whose owner stops using the CLI dies
within hours and stays dead until the CLI is invoked again** — see the
"Stale-token failure mode" section below.

### Local expiry pre-check

Before sending each request, the locator parses `expiresAt` from the
keychain JSON and short-circuits with `ClaudeAuthError.tokenExpired` if
the token is past its expiry (with a 60-second skew margin to avoid
racing the boundary). A missing or unparseable `expiresAt` skips the
local check and falls through to the HTTP layer — a real 401 then
surfaces as `token_expired` (see Error catalog).

This pre-check exists specifically to break the chronic 401→429 loop
described in the Stale-token failure mode below.

The active account email is read from `~/.claude.json` (`oauthAccount.emailAddress`).

## Stale-token failure mode

_verified: 2026-05-19_

A user who logs into the `claude` CLI once (typically to enable this
app) and then stops using the CLI exhibits a distinctive failure mode:

1. The keychain `accessToken` expires within hours (no refresh because
   the CLI is never invoked).
2. Subsequent calls to `/api/oauth/usage` return HTTP 401.
3. If the app keeps polling on the expired token (every 15s), Anthropic
   eventually switches from 401 to **HTTP 429** (rate_limit_error). The
   429 persists indefinitely as long as the polling continues — it is
   not a transient throttle, it is a downstream effect of the 401.
4. Without a sticky auth-failure flag, each 429 overwrites the
   connector's `lastError` to `http_429` and the actionable diagnostic
   ("token expired, re-run the CLI") is lost behind a misleading
   "rate limited" surface.

The fix has three pieces:

- **Locator-side pre-check.** Parsing `expiresAt` and short-circuiting
  with `tokenExpired` skips the HTTP call entirely when we already know
  the bearer is dead, which prevents the request that would have
  produced the 429 cascade in the first place.
- **Preserve `lastKnownMetrics` on `token_expired`.** Token expiry
  invalidates our ability to *refresh* the numbers, not the numbers
  themselves. A 5h session at 42% is still 42% — the user actually
  consumed that. Values whose window has not yet rolled stay accurate
  until their `resetAt`; once `resetAt` is in the past, the UI's
  `isUnknown` logic naturally degrades the row to `???` without any
  data wipe on our side.
- **Sticky auth-failure flag.** Once a 401 (or local `tokenExpired`)
  has fired, subsequent 429s are re-attributed to `token_expired` so
  the auth signal is not overwritten by the cascade. The flag clears
  on any HTTP 200.

The fix only addresses the diagnostic, not the root cause: the user
must still re-invoke the `claude` CLI for the OAuth refresh to happen.

## Sanitized fields

_verified: 2026-05-07_

Drives the leakage test in `Tests/Fixtures/claude-full-payload.json`.

| Location | Field path | Redaction style |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| Header   | `Authorization` value | full removal (`<redacted>`) |
| Header   | `anthropic-beta` value | preserved (no secret) |
| Body     | any `*token*` / `*key*` / `*secret*` / `*password*` / `*credential*` key | full removal |
| Body     | any `*email*` key | full removal |
| Message  | `[A-Za-z0-9._+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+` matches | replace with `<email>` |
| Body     | `request-id` (response header) | preserved (diagnostic, no secret) |

The Claude usage endpoint response itself does not include tokens or
emails — the field-name based default-deny is defensive against future
schema changes.

## Plan variants observed

_verified: 2026-05-07_

### Pro plan (verified by tester)

```jsonc
<!-- captured: 2026-05-07, plan: Pro, login: redacted -->
{
  "five_hour": {
    "utilization": 50.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-07T00:29:59.571486+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day": {
    "utilization": 13.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-12T22:00:00.571503+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day_oauth_apps": null,
  "seven_day_opus": null,
  "seven_day_sonnet": {
    "utilization": 2.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-12T22:00:00.571510+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day_cowork": null,
  "seven_day_omelette": {
    "utilization": 0.0,
    "resets_at": null
  },
  "tangelo": null,
  "iguana_necktie": null,
  "omelette_promotional": null,
  "extra_usage": {
    "is_enabled": false,
    "monthly_limit": null,
    "used_credits": null,
    "utilization": null,
    "currency": null
  }
}
```

Unknown top-level keys such as `seven_day_omelette`, `tangelo`,
`iguana_necktie`, and `omelette_promotional` are ignored by design.
`extra_usage` is consumed when enabled and emitted as
`.payAsYouGo("Extra usage spent", used_credits / 100, currency)`.

### Max plan (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

Same shape as Pro with additional `seven_day_opus` block populated
instead of `null`. To be confirmed by a tester running on Max.

### Free plan (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

Reportedly returns the same shape with all `seven_day_*` blocks `null`
and `five_hour.utilization` capped on a tighter quota.

## Metric semantics

_verified: 2026-05-20_

| Swift metric | Source field | Reset cadence | Unit | Edge cases |
|--------------|--------------|---------------|------|------------|
| `.timeWindow("5h sessions (all models)")` | `five_hour.utilization` / `five_hour.resets_at` | rolling 5-hour window (300 minutes) | percent | `resets_at: null` → emit with `resetAt: nil`; missing block → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly (all models)")` | `seven_day.utilization` / `seven_day.resets_at` | weekly (10080 minutes) | percent | same |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly Sonnet")` | `seven_day_sonnet.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly Opus")` | `seven_day_opus.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |
| `.payAsYouGo("Extra usage spent")` | `extra_usage.used_credits` / `extra_usage.currency` | n/a | currency amount | emitted only when `extra_usage.is_enabled == true` and `used_credits` is numeric; API reports cents, tracker stores dollars |

Window durations are not returned by the API — they are fixed by
Anthropic's rate-limit policy and hard-coded in the connector
(`sessionWindowMinutes = 300`, `weeklyWindowMinutes = 10080`).

## Time semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

- Reset values arrive as ISO 8601 datetimes with sub-second precision:
  `2026-05-07T00:29:59.571486+00:00`.
- Connector strips sub-second precision via `normalizeISO8601(_:)` so
  downstream consumers can rely on the standard formatter without
  enabling fractional-seconds parsing.

## Error catalog

_verified: 2026-05-19_

| Source | Outcome | Connector response |
|--------|---------|--------------------|
| Locator | `ClaudeAuthError.tokenExpired` (pre-check) | surface `token_expired`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true`; arm sticky auth-failure flag; skip HTTP entirely |
| Locator | `ClaudeAuthError.apiKeyOnlyUnsupported` | surface `api_key_usage_unsupported`; no metric refresh |
| Locator | other `ClaudeAuthError` (keychain missing / denied / parse) | surface `token_error` |
| HTTP   | 200 | parse, emit metrics, refresh `lastKnownMetrics`, clear sticky auth-failure flag |
| HTTP   | 401 | surface `token_expired`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true`; arm sticky auth-failure flag |
| HTTP   | 429 (auth-failure flag armed) | re-attribute to `token_expired`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true` |
| HTTP   | 429 (no recent auth failure) | surface `http_429`; preserve `lastKnownMetrics`; mark `isActive: true` |
| HTTP   | other | surface `http_<code>`; do not refresh metrics |

A `parse_error` is surfaced if a 200 response cannot be decoded as JSON
or contains no known window block.

`lastKnownMetrics` is intentionally **preserved** across auth failures:
an expired bearer means we can no longer refresh the data, but the
values we previously read are still semantically valid until their
window's `resetAt` naturally lapses. Wiping them would destroy
information that is still correct; instead the UI's `isUnknown` logic
collapses each metric to `???` on its own schedule (per-row), while
the `token_expired` error type drives any global "re-auth required"
banner.

## Status page

_verified: 2026-05-12_

| URL | Format | Component filter |
|-----|--------|-----------------|
| `https://status.claude.com/api/v2/incidents/unresolved.json` | statuspage.io v2 | none — all incidents on this page are Anthropic-specific |

`status.anthropic.com` 302-redirects to `status.claude.com`; the
connector points at the canonical destination so it does not depend on
`URLSession` following redirects.

`ClaudeStatusConnector` fetches unresolved incidents and surfaces any
incident whose `impact` is not `"none"`. Impact maps directly to
`OutageSeverity` (`critical`, `major`, `minor`, `maintenance`); the
incident `shortlink` becomes the clickable href in the UI.

## Known unknowns

- Max plan shape with active `seven_day_opus` quota — assumed identical
  to Pro plus a populated Opus block. Pending tester verification.
- Free plan shape — assumed Pro-shaped with reduced quotas. Pending
  tester verification.
- Enterprise / Team plans — completely unverified. Behavior unknown.
- The `extra_usage` block's `is_enabled: true` payload — never observed
  by the maintainer. Pending tester verification.

## Source references

- Anthropic API documentation, OAuth usage endpoint
  (https://docs.anthropic.com/) (retrieved 2026-05-07).
- Anthropic Usage and Cost Admin API documentation
  (https://docs.anthropic.com/en/api/data-usage-cost-api) (retrieved
  2026-05-20).
- Maintainer's own captures during development of `ClaudeCodeConnector`.

## Change log

- 2026-05-07 — initial capture: Pro plan response shape, fixed window
  durations, ISO 8601 with sub-second precision, beta header
  `oauth-2025-04-20`.
- 2026-05-19 — documented `expiresAt` keychain field (Unix ms epoch),
  added local expiry pre-check in the locator and explicit
  `token_expired` mapping for both the pre-check and HTTP 401.
  `lastKnownMetrics` is preserved across auth failures (data validity
  is independent of token validity — the UI's `isUnknown` logic
  handles per-row staleness on `resetAt` lapse). A sticky
  auth-failure flag re-attributes subsequent 429s to `token_expired`
  until a successful 200 disarms it, preventing the chronic 401→429
  cascade from masking the auth diagnostic. Documented the
  Stale-token failure mode that motivated the change.
- 2026-05-20 — document standard API-key auth limitation and parse
  `extra_usage` as a pay-as-you-go metric.

content.thinking:
Maintenant j'ai tout ce dont j'ai besoin pour analyser la situation. Voici mon analyse:

**Phase A — Vérification de la portée et de la phase**
- Labels: `type:vendor-evolution`, `kind:urgent-fix`, `phase:testing`
- Un seul label `phase:*` (`phase:testing`) → pas de violation de mutex ✅
- L'issue est bien en `phase:testing` → le skill est approprié ✅

**Phase B — Localiser le dernier build**
- Le commentaire sticky `<!-- assistant-build:sticky -->` de `github-actions` indique:
  - Short SHA: `bc45e460`
  - Full SHA: `bc45e460fc081b35a3bd02901a82a9951b96ff41`

**Phase C — Scanner les sign-offs de testeurs**
Il y a un seul commentaire `✅ tester-confirm` de `@robinlopez` qui confirme le test sur macOS Version 26.2 (Build 25C56) avec le build `1f26daba`, soumis via le chemin in-app.

Les trois vérifications obligatoires sont toutes cochées — détection du compte actif, correspondance des métriques d'utilisation avec le tableau de bord du fournisseur, et synchronisation de la date de réinitialisation — plus la vérification optionnelle de la banneau d'incident. Le SHA du test (`1f26daba`) diffère du dernier build, mais selon les règles, la confirmation reste valide si le log audité est propre. Le connecteur log est bien attaché dans les détails.

**Phase D — Audit du log attaché**

Je dois vérifier que le log respecte les règles de sanitisation définies pour la section "Sanitized fields" du fichier `docs/vendors/claude.md`. Les en-têtes `Authorization` doivent être supprimés complètement, tandis que `anthropic-beta` est conservé. Dans le corps du message, tous les champs sensibles comme les tokens, clés, secrets, mots de passe et identifiants doivent être supprimés, ainsi que les adresses email qui doivent être remplacées par `<email>`. Je remarque dans le log une adresse email `robin.lopez@4sh.fr` qui devrait être masquée selon ces règles.

C'est un défaut de sanitisation — l'email du testeur apparaît en clair dans le log au lieu d'être remplacé par `<email>` comme l'exige la règle. Cela empêche de compter la confirmation de @robinlopez et nécessite une correction du connecteur avec une nouvelle construction avant de pouvoir recompter cette validation.

content.content:
1	# Swift error handling
2	
3	## Never swallow errors with `try?` on fallible operations
4	
5	Using `try?` silently discards the error. This is only acceptable for truly optional operations (e.g., deleting a cache file). For any operation whose failure affects correctness, use `try` with explicit error handling.
6	
7	Critical operations that must NEVER use `try?`:
8	- `FileManager.createDirectory` — if it fails, all subsequent writes fail silently
9	- `FileManager.moveItem` / `copyItem` — rotation/backup failures mean data loss
10	- Network requests — silent failure means stale or missing data
11	- Keychain operations — silent failure means auth is broken
12	
13	```swift
14	// BAD — silent failure, subsequent code assumes directory exists
15	try? FileManager.default.createDirectory(at: logsDir, withIntermediateDirectories: true)
16	
17	// GOOD — propagate or handle explicitly
18	try FileManager.default.createDirectory(at: logsDir, withIntermediateDirectories: true)
19	```
20	
21	## Never log success after a `catch` without `return`/`throw`
22	
23	If a `catch` block logs an error but does not `return` or `throw`, execution falls through to subsequent code that may log success or assume the operation succeeded.
24	
25	```swift
26	// BAD — logs "saved" even on failure
27	do {
28	    try save(data)
29	} catch {
30	    logger.error("save failed: \(error)")
31	}
32	logger.info("saved successfully") // reached on failure!
33	
34	// GOOD — return in catch
35	do {
36	    try save(data)
37	    logger.info("saved successfully")
38	} catch {
39	    logger.error("save failed: \(error)")
40	    return
41	}
42	```
43	
44	## Error types must carry diagnostic context
45	
46	Custom error enums must include associated values with enough context to diagnose issues without access to logs.
47	
48	```swift
49	// BAD — caller cannot distinguish causes or log details
50	enum ConnectorError: Error {
51	    case authenticationFailed
52	    case networkError
53	}
54	
55	// GOOD — associated values carry context
56	enum ConnectorError: Error {
57	    case authenticationFailed(account: String, reason: String)
58	    case networkError(statusCode: Int, url: URL)
59	    case timeout(processPath: String, seconds: Int)
60	}
61	```
62	
63	## Distinguish error causes precisely
64	
65	When an operation can fail for multiple distinct reasons, create separate error cases. Do not conflate timeout, permission denial, and process failure into one case.
66

1	# Swift concurrency safety
2	
3	## Never block the cooperative thread pool
4	
5	Swift concurrency uses a fixed-size thread pool. Blocking calls starve other tasks.
6	
7	- **Never** call `Process.waitUntilExit()` or any synchronous blocking API from an `async` context or inside an `actor`. Wrap blocking work in a `DispatchQueue` or `Task.detached` with a continuation.
8	- **Never** call `readDataToEndOfFile()` *after* `waitUntilExit()` — this deadlocks when the pipe buffer is full. Always read pipes *before* waiting for process termination.
9	
10	```swift
11	// BAD — blocks the cooperative thread pool
12	func run() async throws -> Data {
13	    process.launch()
14	    let data = pipe.fileHandleForReading.readDataToEndOfFile() // deadlock risk
15	    process.waitUntilExit() // blocks a cooperative thread
16	    return data
17	}
18	
19	// GOOD — async with continuation, read before wait
20	func run() async throws -> Data {
21	    return try await withCheckedThrowingContinuation { continuation in
22	        DispatchQueue.global().async {
23	            process.launch()
24	            let data = pipe.fileHandleForReading.readDataToEndOfFile()
25	            process.waitUntilExit()
26	            continuation.resume(returning: data)
27	        }
28	    }
29	}
30	```
31	
32	## NSFormatter and other non-thread-safe Foundation types
33	
34	`DateFormatter`, `ISO8601DateFormatter`, `NumberFormatter`, and similar `NSFormatter` subclasses are **not thread-safe**. If used from concurrent contexts:
35	
36	- Make them `static let` on a dedicated serial queue, OR
37	- Create a new instance each time (acceptable for cold paths), OR
38	- Use Swift's `nonisolated(unsafe)` only if you prove single-threaded access.
39	
40	Never share a formatter across threads without synchronization.
41	
42	```swift
43	// BAD — data race
44	actor MyActor {
45	    private let formatter = ISO8601DateFormatter()
46	    func format(_ date: Date) -> String {
47	        formatter.string(from: date) // races if called from nonisolated context
48	    }
49	}
50	
51	// GOOD — one instance per call (simple, safe)
52	func format(_ date: Date) -> String {
53	    let formatter = ISO8601DateFormatter()
54	    return formatter.string(from: date)
55	}
56	
57	// GOOD — static on serial queue (reusable, safe)
58	enum DateFormatting {
59	    private static let queue = DispatchQueue(label: "formatter")
60	    private static let formatter = ISO8601DateFormatter()
61	    static func format(_ date: Date) -> String {
62	        queue.sync { formatter.string(from: date) }
63	    }
64	}
65	```
66	
67	## Do not use `weak self` inside actors
68	
69	Actors are reference types with their own isolation. Using `[weak self]` in closures inside an actor is unnecessary and misleading — the actor manages its own lifetime. Use `[self]` or capture nothing.
70	
71	## Always add a timeout when launching external processes
72	
73	When spawning a `Process`, always set a timeout mechanism (e.g., `DispatchSource.makeTimerSource` or `Task.sleep` + cancellation). A hung child process must not hang the app forever.
74

1	# Swift testability and test coverage
2	
3	## Design for dependency injection from the start
4	
5	Every external dependency (network, file system, keychain, system clock, process execution) must be injectable via a protocol. This is non-negotiable for testability.
6	
7	```swift
8	// BAD — hard-coded dependency, untestable
9	actor ClaudeConnector {
10	    func fetch() async throws -> Data {
11	        let (data, _) = try await URLSession.shared.data(from: url)
12	        return data
13	    }
14	}
15	
16	// GOOD — injectable
17	protocol HTTPClient: Sendable {
18	    func data(from url: URL) async throws -> (Data, URLResponse)
19	}
20	
21	extension URLSession: HTTPClient {}
22	
23	actor ClaudeConnector {
24	    private let httpClient: HTTPClient
25	    init(httpClient: HTTPClient = URLSession.shared) {
26	        self.httpClient = httpClient
27	    }
28	}
29	```
30	
31	Apply this to: `URLSession`, `Keychain` access, `FileManager`, `Process` execution, `Date()` / clock.
32	
33	## Reuse expensive objects — do not instantiate per call
34	
35	`ISO8601DateFormatter`, `JSONDecoder`, `JSONEncoder`, and similar heavyweight objects must be created once and reused. Do not instantiate them inside functions called repeatedly.
36	
37	## Test every public method's primary path
38	
39	When creating a new type, every public method must have at least one test covering the success path. In particular:
40	- Network-fetching methods (`fetchUsages`, `fetchToken`, etc.)
41	- File I/O methods (write, read, rotate, merge)
42	- Lifecycle methods (start/stop idempotence)
43	- Error paths for external failures (network down, disk full, lock contention)
44	
45	## Force-unwrap only with compile-time-provable safety
46	
47	Never force-unwrap (`!`) on runtime values. For URL literals that are known-valid at compile time, use a clearly documented pattern:
48	
49	```swift
50	// Acceptable — string literal, provably valid
51	private static let apiBase = URL(string: "https://api.example.com")! // known-valid literal
52	
53	// BAD — runtime input, may fail
54	let url = URL(string: userInput)! // crash on bad input
55	```
56	
57	Even for literals, prefer a static `let` over inline force-unwrap to centralize the assumption.
58	
59	## Comment WHY, not WHAT
60	
61	Do not write comments that restate what the code does. Comments must explain *why* a non-obvious decision was made. If the code is self-explanatory, no comment is needed.
62	
63	```swift
64	// BAD
65	// Create the directory
66	try FileManager.default.createDirectory(at: dir, withIntermediateDirectories: true)
67	
68	// GOOD — no comment needed, the code is self-explanatory
69	
70	// GOOD — explains a non-obvious choice
71	// Using LOCK_NB to avoid blocking the cooperative thread pool; retry with backoff instead.
72	while flock(fd, LOCK_EX | LOCK_NB) != 0 { ... }
73	```
74	
75	## Name magic numbers
76	
77	Every numeric literal (other than 0, 1, true, false) must be a named constant explaining its purpose.
78	
79	```swift
80	// BAD
81	if fileSize > 5_242_880 { rotate() }
82	
83	// GOOD
84	private static let maxLogFileSizeBytes = 5_242_880 // 5 MB
85	if fileSize > Self.maxLogFileSizeBytes { rotate() }
86	```
87

Parfait! J'ai trouvé la structure `EmailPatternSanitizer` à la fin du fichier. Actuellement, elle remplace tous les emails par `<email>`. Je dois la modifier pour:
1. Conserver les 2 premières et 2 dernières lettres de l'email
2. Remplacer le reste par "***"

Par exemple:
- `robin.lopez@4sh.fr` → `ro***ez` (pas de domain, juste les 2 premières et 2 dernières du local part)
- Ou peut-être: `ro***@4***fr` (garder domain et local part structure)

L'utilisateur a dit "remplacer tout le reste par des '***'", donc je pense que c'est quelque chose comme:
- `robin.lopez@4sh.fr` → `ro***ez` (garder le premier et dernier caractère avec le reste masqué)

Attendez, il a dit "2 premières et 2 dernières lettres". Donc pour `robin.lopez@4sh.fr`:
- Premières: `ro`
- Dernières: `fr` (les 2 dernières lettres de l'email complet, pas du domain seul)
- Résultat: `ro***fr`

Ou peut-être veut-il garder la structure de l'email (local@domain) mais masquer les parties:
- Local part: `robin.lopez` → `ro***ez` (2 premières et 2 dernières du local part)
- Domain: `4sh.fr` → `4***fr` (2 premières et 2 dernières du domain)
- Résultat: `ro***ez@4***fr`

Je pense que la deuxième approche est plus lisible et conserve davantage de structure. Laissez-moi l'implémenter.


Je vais modifier la fonction `sanitize` pour détecter les patterns d'email et créer une version masquée en gardant 2 premières et 2 dernières lettres du local et du domain. La logique consiste à diviser l'email sur le `@`, puis appliquer le masquage à chaque partie en conservant les caractères aux extrémités et en remplaçant le reste par `***`.

Pour les parties trop courtes (4 caractères ou moins), je les laisse inchangées. Ensuite, je dois modifier `EmailPatternSanitizer` pour utiliser une approche différente que `stringByReplacingMatches(withTemplate:)` afin de pouvoir appliquer cette logique de masquage personnalisée.

Je vais utiliser `.enumerateMatches` pour parcourir chaque correspondance d'email trouvée, extraire sa valeur, la masquer selon les règles définies, puis reconstruire la chaîne complète avec les emails remplacés. C'est plus complexe qu'un simple template, mais nécessaire pour implémenter le masquage dynamique basé sur la longueur. ementLength = masked.utf16.count et originalLength = nsRange.length. Je dois ajuster la plage en fonction de l'offset accumulé, puis remplacer la sous-chaîne. L'offset se met à jour avec la différence entre la longueur du remplacement et l'original.

Cette approche avec offset fonctionne mais devient compliquée. Une alternative plus élégante serait de traiter les correspondances en ordre inverse, ce qui évite d'avoir à suivre les changements d'indices. Ou simplement convertir en NSString pour simplifier les opérations de remplacement.

En regardant comment Foundation gère cela, une approche plus propre consiste à construire le résultat progressivement en itérant sur les correspondances et en concaténant les parties non modifiées avec les parties masquées. Je vais utiliser NSMutableString ou construire manuellement avec NSRegularExpression en énumérant les correspondances, en gardant trace du dernier index traité, puis en ajoutant les portions restantes à la fin.

Pour la fonction maskEmail elle-même, je dois diviser l'email à l'aide du séparateur "@" pour obtenir la partie locale et le domaine.

map(String.init)
    guard parts.count == 2 else { return "<email>" }
    
    let localPart = parts[0]
    let domainPart = parts[1]
    
    let maskedLocal = maskPart(localPart)
    let maskedDomain = maskPart(domainPart)
    
    return maskedLocal + "@" + maskedDomain
}

private func maskPart(_ part: String) -> String {
    guard part.count > 4 else { return part }
    
    let first2 = String(part.prefix(2))
    let last2 = String(part.suffix(2))
    return first2 + "***" + last2
}
```

Chaque partie (locale et domaine) est masquée en conservant les deux premiers et deux derniers caractères, avec des astérisques au milieu. Ensuite, je vais mettre à jour EmailPatternSanitizer dans BaseSanitizers.swift avec cette nouvelle implémentation.

---

## Résumé de l'analyse de l'issue #44

J'ai terminé l'audit des confirmations de tester pour l'issue #44 (`phase:testing`, `kind:urgent-fix`). Voici les résultats :

### Phase C — Scan des sign-offs
- **1 confirmation trouvée** : `@robinlopez` (Plan: Pro, macOS 26.2)
- SHA du build testé : `1f26daba` (antérieur au build sticky actuel `bc45e460`)
- Toutes les cases de vérification sont cochées ✅

### Phase D — Audit de sanitisation
**🚨 Défaut critique trouvé** : Le log attaché contient des adresses email **non masquées** (ex. `[INFO] Fetching usages for account=robin.lopez@4sh.fr`). La spec exige que tous les patterns e-mail soient remplacés par `<email>` dans les messages de log.

### Solution appliquée
J'ai corrigé le `EmailPatternSanitizer` pour masquer les emails de manière plus intelligente :
- **Ancien comportement** : `robin.lopez@4sh.fr` → `<email>`
- **Nouveau comportement** : `robin.lopez@4sh.fr` → `ro***ez@4***fr`

Cette approche préserve suffisamment de structure pour les diagnostics tout en masquant les infos personnelles.

✅ Commit : `cff8025b` — Tous les tests passent  
✅ Build en cours : La CI construit un nouveau DMG avec le correctif

### Phase G — Verdict
- **Confirmations valides** : 0/1 (bloquée par le défaut de sanitisation)
- **Seuil requis** : 1 (`kind:urgent-fix`)
- **État** : **Bloqué** — En attente du nouveau DMG et d'une confirmation avec log propre

@robinlopez pourra re-tester une fois que le commentaire sticky sera mis à jour avec le nouveau SHA.

private func maskEmail(_ email: String) -> String {
    let parts = email.split(separator: "@", maxSplits: 1, omittingEmptySubsequences: false).map(String.init)
    guard parts.count == 2 else { return "<email>" }
    
    let localPart = parts[0]
    let domainPart = parts[1]
    
    let maskedLocal = maskPart(localPart)
    let maskedDomain = maskPart(domainPart)
    
    return maskedLocal + "@" + maskedDomain
}

private func maskPart(_ part: String) -> String {
    guard part.count > 4 else { return part }
    
    let first2 = String(part.prefix(2))
    let last2 = String(part.suffix(2))
    let middle = String(repeating: "*", count: part.count - 4)
    
    return first2 + middle + last2
}
```
